The best-fitting (η, γ) we found here describes the average pattern across our 20 brains. It answers the question: what single wiring rule best reproduces the observed networks?
But individuals differ! In the next tutorial, we will run an individual-level sweep to extract one (η, γ) per person, and then ask whether those individual differences correlate with early life stress.

title: Model Specification
text:
Now that we know what a GNM is how it works, we are ready to put all we’ve learned into practice!!!
We have seen that by changing the values of η (eta) and γ (gamma), we can produce synthetic networks that vary in their topology. The goal now is to find the combination of η and γ that produces a synthetic network that looks as much as possible as the real ones. To do that, we must generate networks for each combination of η and γ, and then check which one is more similar to the real brains. We call this process a parameter sweep.
To to this, we will use our fantastic GNM toolbox!!!! If you remember, we have set it up already in the Installation tutorial, so we can now just simply use it as any other python package.
Before getting into all that, let’s import our data.

title: Model Specification
text:
Load the data
We load all 20 preprocessed brains. First-off, we make these networks BINARY. This means that we do not care about how strong the connection is: just whether it is there or not:

0 = the connection is not there
1 = the connection is there!

Let’s do that:
import numpy as np
import torch

# Load the preprocessed (thresholded, connected) networks
brains = np.load("brain_networks_20_preprocessed.npy")
num_brains, num_nodes, _ = brains.shape

# Binarise each brain (connection present = 1, absent = 0)
binary_brains = (brains > 0).astype(float)
binary_brains_tensor = torch.tensor(binary_brains, dtype=torch.float32)

print(f"Loaded {num_brains} brains, each {num_nodes} x {num_nodes}")






Note



You might have noticed we used torch.tensor(). That’s the specific format required by the GNM toolbox - it’s much better and much faster than numpy, that we have used up until now. So let’s give the toolbox what it wants!!!


Next, we load the distance matrix:
dist_np = np.load("distance_matrix.npy")
distance_matrix = torch.tensor(dist_np, dtype=torch.float32)
This matrix simply tells us how far from each other each pair of connections is.

title: Model Specification
text:
Parameter sweep
Perfect! We are now ready to start our parameter sweep. The GNM toolbox makes this (hopefully) very easy: it has TWO function called BinarySweepParameters() and SweepConfig() and that’s all we need.
This function takes in 6 arguments. If we don’t give it these 6 arguments, it will complain! So before typing any code, let’s understand what there arguments are:

The first two arguments are eta and gamma values (I hope you know what these are by now!)
The next two arguments are distance_relationship_type and preferential_relationship_type. These are just default settings we don’t really have to worry about (Remember when we looked at the GNM formula in the last tutorial? eta and gamma were following a powerlaw in there, and so here we’re just simply giving the instruction: follow the standard GNM formula!)
next up, we have generative_rule. Again, here we just want to use the standard rule (homophily) and so we will just tell to the function to use MatchingIndex(), which is the proper name for homophily, if you remember!
And finally, the function wants to know how many connections it needs to generate. We want as many connections as we have in our 20 brains - which, if you remember, have all the same number of connections (same density!)

Amazing, so let’s define all these things and give them to the function:
from gnm import fitting, generative_rules

eta_values   = torch.linspace(-5,  0,   10)
gamma_values = torch.linspace(-0.5, 0.5, 10)
num_connections = int(binary_brains_tensor[0].sum().item() / 2)

binary_sweep_parameters = fitting.BinarySweepParameters(
    eta    = eta_values,
    gamma  = gamma_values,
    distance_relationship_type         = ["powerlaw"],
    preferential_relationship_type     = ["powerlaw"],
    generative_rule  = [generative_rules.MatchingIndex()],
    num_iterations   = [num_connections],
)
So we defined 10 values for η and 10 values for γ: 100 combinations in total. And we took as many connections as we have in our brain networks. For all the other parameters, we just put the default values we have discussed, so we don’t have to worry about those anymore.
Perfect! Now that we defined our parameters sweep, it’s time to set just some more details, and we need our second function for that, SweepConfig(). Here, we have to specify:

what are the parameters to sweep over (we’ve just defined them!)
the distance matrix (easy, we have already imported that one!)
how many simulations we want. That’s a very important one. GNMs are probabilistic, which means that the same exact combination of parameters might STILL produce different final synthetic networks. So it’s quite risky to produce just one model for each combination of parameters: what if it’s a weird one that we just got by chance? To be more confident that our synthetic networks are reliable, we produce many of them!

So here’s the function and its arguments:
sweep_config = fitting.SweepConfig(
    binary_sweep_parameters = binary_sweep_parameters,
    num_simulations         = 10,
    distance_matrix         = [distance_matrix],
)
Perfect, everything has been specified!!! Have you ever heard of parameter space? It sounds very fancy. It means all the potential combination of different parameters you want in your models. And you’ve just created one! Congrats for defining your first GNM parameter space!






Important



You might have noticed that here we vary only two parameters: eta and gamma. For those two, we specified multiple values. For all the other parameters, we only specified a fixed default. However, all parameters can be changed! For example we may want to test out other wiring rules, different from homophily. Maybe something like: brain areas are more likely to wire together IF their genes are more similar!
Once you figure our the basic workings of GNMs, possibilities to personalize them and explore new things are endless!

title: Individual Differences
text:
In the last tutorial we found the combination of η and γ that best describes the average brain across all 20 participants. But that’s just the beginning of the story!
Think about it: we’ve been summarising 20 different brains with a single pair of numbers. In reality, each brain follows its own wiring rules. Some people might have a stronger distance penalty, meaning their brains are built more locally. Others might show stronger homophily, meaning their brains have tighter clusters. And if those individual differences in wiring rules are meaningful, they might relate to something biologically important.
If you remember from the Research Question tutorial, the central prediction of the Adaptive Stochasticity Hypothesis is that brains shaped by adversity develop in a more stochastic, less constrained way. So our research question is: do people who experienced more early life stress end up having brains wired differently?
Now we can finally tackle it head on!

title: Individual Differences
text:
The plan
The approach is straightforward:

Take the group optimum we found in the last tutorial as our starting point
Run a new, finer sweep centred on that optimum
For each brain, extract the (η, γ) that best fits that individual
Correlate those individual estimates with early life stress scores

The key difference from the last tutorial is step 3: instead of averaging energy across all brains and picking one winner, we let each brain pick its own winner. Let’s walk through it!

title: Individual Differences
text:
Load the data
We start exactly as before: load the 20 preprocessed brains and the distance matrix. This time we also load the early life stress scores, one per participant.
import numpy as np
import torch

brains = np.load("brain_networks_20_preprocessed.npy")
num_brains, num_nodes, _ = brains.shape
binary_brains = (brains > 0).astype(float)
binary_brains_tensor = torch.tensor(binary_brains, dtype=torch.float32)

dist_np = np.load("distance_matrix.npy")
distance_matrix = torch.tensor(dist_np, dtype=torch.float32)

stress_scores = np.load("stress_scores.npy")

num_connections = int(binary_brains_tensor[0].sum().item() / 2)
print(f"Loaded {num_brains} brains and {num_brains} stress scores")

title: Individual Differences
text:
Find the group optimum
We then load the group-level experiments we saved in the last tutorial, and extract the group optimum. We need it to centre our new, finer sweep!
import pickle
import numpy as np
from gnm import evaluation

with open("experiments.pkl", "rb") as f:
    group_experiments = pickle.load(f)

criteria = [
    evaluation.DegreeKS(),
    evaluation.BetweennessKS(),
    evaluation.ClusteringKS(),
    evaluation.EdgeLengthKS(distance_matrix),
]
energy = evaluation.MaxCriteria(criteria)
energy_key = str(energy)

mean_energies = [
    float(exp.evaluation_results.binary_evaluations[energy_key].mean())
    for exp in group_experiments
]

best_idx    = int(np.argmin(mean_energies))
best_exp    = group_experiments[best_idx]
group_eta   = float(best_exp.run_config.binary_parameters.eta)
group_gamma = float(best_exp.run_config.binary_parameters.gamma)

print(f"Group optimum: eta = {group_eta:.2f}, gamma = {group_gamma:.2f}")

title: Individual Differences
text:
The fine-grained sweep
Now for the new sweep. The group sweep covered a wide region of the parameter space (η from -5 to 0, γ from -0.5 to 0.5). For individual fitting, we do something smarter: we zoom in on the region around the group optimum.
Why is this better? Two reasons:

Precision: a finer grid means we can pick up subtle differences between individuals that would be invisible on a coarse grid.
Speed: by covering a smaller range, we can afford more grid points and more simulations, giving us more reliable estimates.

We also run more simulations than before — remember, GNMs are probabilistic, so more simulations means more stable estimates for each individual:
from gnm import fitting, generative_rules

n_eta   = 15
n_gamma = 15
n_sims  = 20

eta_values   = torch.linspace(group_eta   - 2.0, group_eta   + 2.0, n_eta).clamp(-7, 0)
gamma_values = torch.linspace(group_gamma - 0.3, group_gamma + 0.3, n_gamma).clamp(-1, 2)

binary_sweep_parameters = fitting.BinarySweepParameters(
    eta    = eta_values,
    gamma  = gamma_values,
    distance_relationship_type         = ["powerlaw"],
    preferential_relationship_type     = ["powerlaw"],
    generative_rule  = [generative_rules.MatchingIndex()],
    num_iterations   = [num_connections],
)

sweep_config = fitting.SweepConfig(
    binary_sweep_parameters = binary_sweep_parameters,
    num_simulations         = n_sims,
    distance_matrix         = [distance_matrix],
)
We then run the sweep in exactly the same way as before:
import time

start = time.perf_counter()

experiments = fitting.perform_sweep(
    sweep_config         = sweep_config,
    binary_evaluations   = [energy],
    real_binary_matrices = binary_brains_tensor,
    save_model           = False,
    save_run_history     = False,
)

elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"Sweep complete in {elapsed:.1f} s")






NoteHow long will this take?



With 15 × 15 = 225 parameter combinations × 20 simulations = 4500 model runs. This is more than the group sweep, but still manageable on a CPU. Download the pre-computed results below to skip the wait!
⬇ Download individual_experiments.pkl
with open("individual_experiments.pkl", "rb") as f:
    experiments = pickle.load(f)

title: Individual Differences
text:
Extracting individual estimates
Here is where the magic happens. Instead of taking the mean energy across all brains and finding one global winner, we now ask: for each brain separately, which (η, γ) gives the lowest energy?
The GNM toolbox has a function for exactly this: optimise_evaluation(). You’ve seen it before! But now, instead of thinking about it as a way to find the best overall configuration, notice what it actually returns: one experiment per brain — specifically, the parameter configuration that best fits that individual.
optimal_experiments, optimal_energies = fitting.optimise_evaluation(
    experiments = experiments,
    criterion   = energy,
)
Now we just read off the (η, γ) from each brain’s best-fitting experiment:
import pandas as pd

rows = []
for brain_idx, (opt_exp, opt_energy) in enumerate(zip(optimal_experiments, optimal_energies)):
    rows.append({
        "brain":       brain_idx,
        "eta":         float(opt_exp.run_config.binary_parameters.eta),
        "gamma":       float(opt_exp.run_config.binary_parameters.gamma),
        "best_energy": float(opt_energy),
        "stress":      float(stress_scores[brain_idx]),
    })

df_ind = pd.DataFrame(rows)
print(df_ind)
We now have a dataframe with one row per participant, telling us their individual η, γ, best energy, and stress score.

title: Individual Differences
text:
Correlating with early life stress
Now for the key question: do individual differences in wiring rules relate to early life stress?
We test this with a simple Pearson correlation — the same kind we used in the Topology tutorial. We check both parameters separately:
from scipy import stats

r_eta,   p_eta   = stats.pearsonr(df_ind["stress"], df_ind["eta"])
r_gamma, p_gamma = stats.pearsonr(df_ind["stress"], df_ind["gamma"])

print(f"eta   vs stress: r = {r_eta:.2f},  p = {p_eta:.3f}")
print(f"gamma vs stress: r = {r_gamma:.2f}, p = {p_gamma:.3f}")
And we visualise the results with scatter plots:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

BLUE = "#2E86AB"

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

for ax, param, r_val, p_val, ylabel in zip(
    axes,
    ["eta",               "gamma"],
    [r_eta,               r_gamma],
    [p_eta,               p_gamma],
    ["η (distance penalty)", "γ (homophily)"],
):
    ax.scatter(df_ind["stress"], df_ind[param], color=BLUE, s=70, zorder=3)
    m, b = np.polyfit(df_ind["stress"], df_ind[param], 1)
    xs = np.linspace(df_ind["stress"].min(), df_ind["stress"].max(), 100)
    ax.plot(xs, m * xs + b, color="red", lw=1.8)
    ax.set_xlabel("Early life stress score", fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel(ylabel, fontsize=12)
    ax.set_title(f"r = {r_val:.2f},  p = {p_val:.3f}", fontsize=13)
    ax.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("../images/GenerativeModels/individual_correlations.png", dpi=150)
plt.show()

title: Individual Differences
text:
What do the results mean?
The two panels tell us whether early life stress is linked to differences in wiring rules. Let’s think about what each parameter means biologically:
A correlation with η would mean that people with more early life stress have a different spatial wiring strategy. A more negative η means the brain is more strongly constrained by distance — it mostly connects nearby regions. A less negative η means the brain is more willing to form long-range connections. So if stress correlates with η, it would suggest that early adversity changes how much the developing brain “cares about” physical distance when laying down its connections.
A correlation with γ would mean that stress is linked to differences in homophily — how strongly the brain’s wiring builds on itself, connecting regions that already share neighbours. Stronger homophily builds tighter, more segregated modules; weaker homophily produces a more integrated, less clustered network. A stress-related shift in γ would suggest that adversity affects how modular the brain becomes during development.






ImportantWhat if the correlations are not significant?



That is a completely valid outcome! Remember: we’ve been working with only 20 brains, which gives us limited statistical power. Real studies in this area typically use hundreds of participants. With 20 people, you would need a very strong correlation to reach significance.
What matters here is understanding the logic: we’ve moved from describing the brain with a handful of topology metrics to describing it with two interpretable wiring parameters. And whether the result is significant or not, we’re asking a much richer question than we could with topology alone.








NoteFrom two numbers to a whole story



It’s worth stepping back and appreciating what we’ve done across these tutorials. We started with raw connectivity matrices and ended up with two numbers per person that capture how their brain wires itself. Those numbers are not arbitrary: they describe real developmental processes, they have a biological interpretation, and they can be compared across individuals and related to life experiences.
That’s the power of Generative Network Models!

title: Individual Differences
text:
Our 20 brains
Let’s start by reminding ourselves of what we are working with. Here are the 20 brain networks we have been working with throughout these tutorials:





Each panel shows a different individual’s connectivity matrix. They all look similar at a glance, but the differences between them are exactly what we want to capture. The key question is: are those differences related to early life stress?

title: Individual Differences
text:
From group to individual
In the last tutorial, we averaged energy across all 20 brains to find the single best-fitting (η, γ). Now we want to be more specific: for each brain separately, we pick the parameter configuration that gave that brain the lowest energy.
We don’t need to run a new sweep for this! All the info is already in the experiments from last time. Let’s load them:
import pickle
import numpy as np
import torch
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from collections import Counter

with open("experiments.pkl", "rb") as f:
    group_experiments = pickle.load(f)
For each brain, we find the combination of parameters that leads to the lowest (the best) energy by averaging over simulations (there is some extra code to rebuild the group-level landscape so we can use it for background colors in the plot):
# Per-brain coarse optima
per_brain_energy  = torch.stack(
    [exp['energy_tensor'].mean(dim=0) for exp in group_experiments]
)  # [n_configs, n_brains]
best_config_per_brain = per_brain_energy.argmin(dim=0)

ind_eta_c   = [group_experiments[int(idx)]['eta']   for idx in best_config_per_brain]
ind_gamma_c = [group_experiments[int(idx)]['gamma'] for idx in best_config_per_brain]

# Rebuild landscape and group optimum for plotting
mean_energies_group = [float(exp['energy_tensor'].mean()) for exp in group_experiments]
rows_group = [{"eta": exp['eta'], "gamma": exp['gamma'], "energy": e}
              for exp, e in zip(group_experiments, mean_energies_group)]
landscape   = pd.DataFrame(rows_group).pivot(index="eta", columns="gamma", values="energy")
best_idx    = int(np.argmin(mean_energies_group))
group_eta   = group_experiments[best_idx]['eta']
group_gamma = group_experiments[best_idx]['gamma']

counts = Counter(zip(ind_eta_c, ind_gamma_c))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
im = ax.imshow(
    landscape.values,
    origin="lower", aspect="auto", cmap="viridis_r",
    extent=[
        landscape.columns.min(), landscape.columns.max(),
        landscape.index.min(),   landscape.index.max(),
    ],
)
for (eta_val, gamma_val), n in counts.items():
    ax.scatter(gamma_val, eta_val, marker="x", s=140, color="white",
               linewidths=2.5, zorder=5)
    if n > 1:
        ax.text(gamma_val + 0.02, eta_val + 0.1, str(n),
                color="white", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold", zorder=6)
ax.scatter(group_gamma, group_eta, s=150, color="red", zorder=7, label="Group optimum")
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label="Energy (max KS)")
ax.set_xlabel("gamma (homophily)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("eta (distance penalty)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("Individual optima on coarse grid", fontsize=13)
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("../images/GenerativeModels/individual_landscape_coarse.png", dpi=150)
plt.show()





Do you see the problem? The red numbers next to the Xs show how many participants are stacked at the exact same point! With a coarse 10×10 grid, many individuals end up sharing the same (η, γ) even though their true optima may be slightly different. We are losing precision.

title: Individual Differences
text:
A finer sweep
To fix this, we run a second, more targeted sweep. Looking at the coarse landscape, the interesting action is clearly happening in a narrower region: roughly η between −3 and −0.5, and γ between 0 and 0.5. So that’s exactly where we sweep:






ImportantChoose the sweep range carefully!



There is no automatic rule for picking the fine-sweep range. We chose η ∈ [−3, −0.5] and γ ∈ [0, 0.5] by eye, based on where the coarse landscape placed most individual optima. For your own dataset, you will need to do the same inspection.
One thing that is critical: after extracting the per-brain optima, make sure no participant ends up with their best point right on the border of the parameter space. If someone’s best (η, γ) is at the minimum or maximum value you tested, their true optimum might be beyond that boundary and you are artificially clamping them. When this happens, widen the range in that direction and re-run.


For this we need to load the brain networks, distance matrix (and, since we’ll need them later, the stress scores too):
import numpy as np
import torch

brains = np.load("brain_networks_20_preprocessed.npy")
num_brains, num_nodes, _ = brains.shape
binary_brains = (brains > 0).astype(float)
binary_brains_tensor = torch.tensor(binary_brains, dtype=torch.float32)

dist_np = np.load("distance_matrix.npy")
distance_matrix = torch.tensor(dist_np, dtype=torch.float32)

stress_scores = np.load("stress_scores.npy")
num_connections = int(binary_brains_tensor[0].sum().item() / 2)
from gnm import fitting, generative_rules, evaluation

criteria = [
    evaluation.DegreeKS(),
    evaluation.BetweennessKS(),
    evaluation.ClusteringKS(),
    evaluation.EdgeLengthKS(distance_matrix),
]
energy = evaluation.MaxCriteria(criteria)

n_eta   = 30
n_gamma = 30
n_sims  = 30

eta_values   = torch.linspace(-3.0, -0.5, n_eta)
gamma_values = torch.linspace( 0.0,  0.5, n_gamma)

binary_sweep_parameters = fitting.BinarySweepParameters(
    eta    = eta_values,
    gamma  = gamma_values,
    distance_relationship_type         = ["powerlaw"],
    preferential_relationship_type     = ["powerlaw"],
    generative_rule  = [generative_rules.MatchingIndex()],
    num_iterations   = [num_connections],
)
sweep_config = fitting.SweepConfig(
    binary_sweep_parameters = binary_sweep_parameters,
    num_simulations         = n_sims,
    distance_matrix         = [distance_matrix],
)
We increased n_eta and n_gamma to 30 each (instead of 10), and we also increased n_sims to 30. More simulations mean more stable estimates for each individual, which is important when we’re trying to detect subtle differences between people.
import time

start = time.perf_counter()

raw_fine = fitting.perform_sweep(
    sweep_config         = sweep_config,
    binary_evaluations   = [energy],
    real_binary_matrices = binary_brains_tensor,
    save_model           = False,
    save_run_history     = False,
)

elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"Sweep complete in {elapsed:.1f} s")

# Convert to plain dicts (same format as the group experiments)
energy_key = str(energy)
experiments_fine = [
    {
        "eta":           float(exp.run_config.binary_parameters.eta),
        "gamma":         float(exp.run_config.binary_parameters.gamma),
        "energy_tensor": exp.evaluation_results.binary_evaluations[energy_key],
    }
    for exp in raw_fine
]






NoteHow long will this take?



30 × 30 = 900 parameter combinations × 30 simulations = 27 000 model runs. On a normal laptop, you may want to run it before leaving work and come back for the results the morning after!
So for now, download the pre-computed results below!
⬇ Download individual_experiments.pkl
import pickle

with open("individual_experiments.pkl", "rb") as f:
    experiments_fine = pickle.load(f)

title: Individual Differences
text:
Checking the fine-grid optima
Let’s extract the individual optima from the fine sweep and verify that participants are now spread across distinct grid points:
# Per-brain fine optima: same logic as before
per_brain_fine   = torch.stack(
    [exp['energy_tensor'].mean(dim=0) for exp in experiments_fine]
)  # [n_configs, n_brains]
best_config_fine = per_brain_fine.argmin(dim=0)  # [n_brains]

ind_eta_f   = [experiments_fine[int(idx)]['eta']   for idx in best_config_fine]
ind_gamma_f = [experiments_fine[int(idx)]['gamma'] for idx in best_config_fine]

counts_fine = Counter(zip(ind_eta_f, ind_gamma_f))
print(f"Unique fine optima: {len(counts_fine)} out of {num_brains}")
mean_energies_fine = [float(exp['energy_tensor'].mean()) for exp in experiments_fine]
rows_fine   = [
    {"eta": exp['eta'], "gamma": exp['gamma'], "energy": e}
    for exp, e in zip(experiments_fine, mean_energies_fine)
]
df_fine        = pd.DataFrame(rows_fine)
landscape_fine = df_fine.pivot(index="eta", columns="gamma", values="energy")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
im = ax.imshow(
    landscape_fine.values,
    origin="lower", aspect="auto", cmap="viridis_r",
    extent=[
        landscape_fine.columns.min(), landscape_fine.columns.max(),
        landscape_fine.index.min(),   landscape_fine.index.max(),
    ],
)
for (eta_val, gamma_val), n in counts_fine.items():
    ax.scatter(gamma_val, eta_val, marker="x", s=140, color="white",
               linewidths=2.5, zorder=5)
    if n > 1:
        ax.text(gamma_val + 0.006, eta_val + 0.05, str(n),
                color="white", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold", zorder=6)
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label="Energy (max KS)")
ax.set_xlabel("gamma (homophily)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("eta (distance penalty)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("Individual optima on fine grid", fontsize=13)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("../images/GenerativeModels/individual_landscape_fine.png", dpi=150)
plt.show()





Much better! The individual optima are now spread across distinct grid points. We can actually see the individual differences we were after! You can notice that there are still a couple of individuals with the same values, but that’s ok! After all, some people might actually have similar brains!

title: Individual Differences
text:
Build the individual dataframe
Now we collect everything into a tidy dataframe: one row per participant, with their individual η, γ, energy, and stress score:
rows_ind = []
for brain_idx, idx in enumerate(best_config_fine):
    best_exp = experiments_fine[int(idx)]
    rows_ind.append({
        "brain":       brain_idx,
        "eta":         best_exp['eta'],
        "gamma":       best_exp['gamma'],
        "best_energy": float(best_exp['energy_tensor'].mean(dim=0)[brain_idx]),
        "stress":      float(stress_scores[brain_idx]),
    })

df_ind = pd.DataFrame(rows_ind)
print(df_ind)

title: Individual Differences
section: Do the wiring rules relate to early life stress?
text:
Do the wiring rules relate to early life stress?
Now the big question. Just like we did in the Topology tutorial, we correlate each parameter with the stress scores using a Pearson correlation and visualise the result with a regression plot:
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats

r_eta,   p_eta   = stats.pearsonr(df_ind["stress"], df_ind["eta"])
r_gamma, p_gamma = stats.pearsonr(df_ind["stress"], df_ind["gamma"])

COLORS = ["#C0392B", "#2980B9"]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

for ax, param, r_val, p_val, ylabel, color in zip(
    axes,
    ["eta",                 "gamma"],
    [r_eta,                 r_gamma],
    [p_eta,                 p_gamma],
    ["η (distance penalty)", "γ (homophily)"],
    COLORS,
):
    sns.regplot(
        x=df_ind["stress"], y=df_ind[param],
        ax=ax,
        scatter_kws={"color": color, "edgecolors": "white",
                     "linewidths": 0.6, "s": 60, "zorder": 3},
        line_kws={"color": color, "linewidth": 2},
        ci=95,
        color=color,
    )
    p_str = f"p = {p_val:.3f}" if p_val >= 0.001 else "p < 0.001"
    ax.set_title(f"r = {r_val:+.2f},  {p_str}", fontsize=12)
    ax.set_xlabel("Early life stress score", fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel(ylabel, fontsize=12)
    ax.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)

fig.suptitle("GNM Parameters vs. Early Life Stress", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("../images/GenerativeModels/individual_correlations.png", dpi=150)
plt.show()





Both the correlations are significant!!! Early life stress is linked to individual differences in wiring rules!!
A correlation with η means that people with more early life stress have a different spatial wiring strategy. A less negative η means the brain is more willing to form long-range connections, which is consistent with the Adaptive Stochasticity Hypothesis: adversity might loosen the distance constraint, allowing for a more diffuse, stochastic developmental process.
A correlation with γ means that stress is linked to differences in homophily. Weaker homophily produces a more integrated, less clustered network and this is again consistent with a more stochastic developmental programme that does not lock in tight local communities.






ImportantGNMs are playing a different game



This is just an example! What matters here is understanding the logic: we have moved from describing the brain with a handful of topology metrics to capturing its wiring principles with two interpretable parameters. We are asking a much richer and more mechanistic question than we could with topology alone.

title: Individual Differences
text:
Summary
It’s worth stepping back and appreciating what we’ve done across these tutorials. We started with raw connectivity matrices and ended up with two numbers per person that capture how their brain wires itself. Those numbers can be compared across individuals and related to life experiences.
That’s the power of Generative Network Models!
